evidence: Package decomposition maps to domain capabilities: config/ for grammar loading, model/ for domain types, api/ for HTTP, exploration/ for semantic algorithms, query/ for corpus access, utils/ for shared primitives.
GrammarConfig and GrammarConfigLoader are cleanly separated — the loader owns file I/O, the config owns the data. Callers accept GrammarConfig directly without needing the loader.
model/ package is self-contained: it has no imports from api/ or exploration/. It is a pure domain package.
The handler split (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, BcqlHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) correctly separates endpoint concerns so each file owns a coherent surface.

RadialPlot in api/ was moved in r17 to co-locate with VisualizationHandlers, but RadialPlot generates SVG and has no HTTP-specific logic — its ownership story blurs the 'api/' contract for a new engineer.

QueryResults resides in model/ but is produced by query/ and consumed most heavily by api/ and exploration/ — the package signal (model/) says 'domain data' but the production origin is 'query output'.
exploration/ and query/ are separate packages with no coupling between them: exploration consumes query results but doesn't directly invoke query logic.

strengths: Top-level package names map to domain capabilities rather than historical accidents (exploration/, query/, config/ are self-explanatory).
GrammarConfig / GrammarConfigLoader separation is a textbook loader/data split — callers hold only what they need.
model/ is a stable, import-free domain package that any module can depend on without pulling in higher-layer concerns.
The handler decomposition in api/ makes it immediately clear which HTTP surface each class owns.
exploration/ and query/ are decoupled — the semantic algorithm does not know about corpus internals.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The two placement issues (RadialPlot in api/, QueryResults in model/) both blur the ownership story for a new engineer — they are isolated misplacements rather than a systemic decomposition problem.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase has a coherent, intentional top-level structure that maps cleanly to domain capabilities. A new engineer can understand why each package exists and roughly predict what it contains. The score is held below 85 by two placement anomalies: RadialPlot (a rendering component) in api/ and QueryResults (a query-layer output type) in model/. These are navigability friction points rather than deep structural failures.

issues.summary: RadialPlot (SVG renderer) lives in api/ but has no HTTP-specific logic
evidence: RadialPlot.java contains only SVG generation logic with no HttpExchange, no headers, no status codes.
VisualizationHandlers.java is the HTTP adapter that calls RadialPlot.renderFromItems(...) and sends the response — a clean two-level split exists but both live in api/.
A new engineer reading api/ would expect HTTP handlers; finding an SVG layout class with spiral constants breaks that expectation.
issues.suggestion: Move RadialPlot to a visualization/ package (or api/viz/). VisualizationHandlers stays in api/. The rendering logic is then correctly separated from the HTTP adapter.

issues.summary: QueryResults lives in model/ but belongs conceptually to the query/ package
evidence: QueryResults is returned by all QueryExecutor methods; it is the output type of the query layer.
model/ is documented as a 'domain model' package, but QueryResults (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, SnippetResult) are corpus-query output structures, not domain concepts.
Moving QueryResults to query/ would make the QueryExecutor interface self-contained and would clarify that model/ holds only semantic-domain types.
issues.suggestion: Move QueryResults.java to the query/ package alongside QueryExecutor. Update imports in api/ and exploration/. This aligns production origin with package membership.

root_causes: Incremental file additions to existing packages without periodic placement review
